speaker: interviewer
text: July the 14th, 1995-- the survivor is Cipora Nutovits-- Ellen Baskin, interviewer-- Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 14, 1995. The survivor is Cipora Nutovits. My name is Ellen Baskin. We're in Los Angeles, California, in the United States of America. And the language is English. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Sh!

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number one with Cipora Nutovits. I'd like to begin by asking you to tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Cipora Nutovits. From childhood, I am Gezella Spitzer, or Spitzer, Gezella.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell both of those for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cipora Nutovits-- C-I-P-O-R-A N-U-T-O-V-T-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name from Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name from Hungary is Spitzer-- S-P-I-- Spitzer-- T-Z-E-R. And Gezella is G-E-Z-E-L-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does Cipora mean in Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cipora is mean is a bird. And I like to be a bird, because I like to be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I born in Hungarian, in Hadjunanas, in August 9, '33.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am going to be 62 in August.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' name was Avram Spitzer [NON-ENGLISH] and Rachel Spitzer, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your family in Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungaria, we was a very big family. My mother raised 15 children. But this-- and I have six children-- six brothers and sisters-- halfs brothers and sisters. But I have seven-- seven-- six sisters-- six sisters from my mother-- from my-- from my mother and-- and father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father was married once before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he-- my father was married, and he had six children. When the-- when the wife was pregnant, he was died. And he-- he take. And he took the child too. So my father left with six children. And later, my mother went married to him. And when he-- when she married, she has a nine children-- two was dead, and the other was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you of the seven children who lived that your mother gave birth to? Were you born first, third?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am the little children. I-- I am between the little children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- [LAUGHS] --Hannah, the first, Yeshi-- Yeshi-- and I-- I was-- I have [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was died-- David, and it was another child that he die. I don't know it-- he was-- she was a baby. I don't know. He was very, very young. And later, I came. And we-- I have a brother and a sister-- twins. This is Mark and Joseph. And the little-- the little one is Esther. She was born. She was the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a shochet. He was a shochet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is a shochet?

speaker: interviewee
text: A shochet is-- the people went a very special place to take a chicken or a-- and he need to kill the-- the chicken or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the kosher meat.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the kosher meat, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was orthodox, yes. It was [NON-ENGLISH] orthodox-- very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the Jewish community like in the town that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was very orthodox, all of them. And we have a lot of people over there-- a lot of families. And all the families has a lot of babies-- and not only one-- everybody has about 9, 10 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your earliest memory from your childhood there-- the very first thing you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a very little memory that I went to the nursery. I think I was-- I don't know-- perhaps 4. And I went with-- with another sister. She must 2 and 2 a 1/2. I don't remember. I don't remember. This was the first memory. And I-- I remember is the nursery, that we were sitting like this. And the teacher was sitting far from us. We were sitting in a room. It was almost one ball and one puppet-- and was sitting. And she was telling all kind of stories. We cannot move. We were sitting. And we can't play only outside. We have a very big yard outside. We have-- we have swings. And I remember the swings. I remember that I was-- played with my sister. And I don't remember the teachers. I don't remember the-- the-- the children. But I remember that the nursery was not Jewish place. This was gentile's place. But I don't know why my mother took us over there. And it was very-- like I am know-- I was-- I know I am a teacher. And I know the children are moving and playing. And they can do whatever what-- what we want. Over there, we were sitting hours, hours, like now I sitting now. This I remember-- the first picture. What-- what happened at home, I don't remember. But I remember that my mother and father like a lot of watch-- watches-- on the-- on the-- on the wall. And we have a very old, very nice watch. And every time it was-- the watch was moving-- you know-- not moving every time-- every 1/2 hour and every hour it was-- tells the time-- you know-- 1:00, 2:00, 3:00. And I remember this, even in the night. And this give me-- until now I love watches. And I have a lot of watches. And my husband like too. And it is-- it give me a very good feeling that I-- I hear watches. Until now, I have in the kitchen a very special watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: A clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: A clock-- but my mother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --was sleeping. When she was going to sleep, he was-- take the watch with her in the kitchen. I loved this very much. [CRYING] And I love my mother very much. [CRYING] I think of her very, very much-- my husband, the children-- [CRYING] And everybody love her. She was a very special woman. [CRYING] She died almost 95 years-- 95. She was by us 20 years. My husband take care of-- very nice. And my children take-- love her very much. [CRYING] She passed in-- in '87. I was in Israel before she was very sick. I spend with her more [NON-ENGLISH] I came to her, and he was very sick. And I wash her. I make her the bed. I feed her. I give her every-- and I-- I take only two-- two days off. I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I love her very much. He was a very special, very good woman. She was helping for everybody. I have very hard-- I have a headache.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] OK, if we could go back to the time before the war in Hungary. What were the relations like between the Jews and the non-Jews when you were growing up there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Even when I was little-- well, I don't know-- '42 or '40-- when I remember-- it was a-- we have-- we have alone-- in our town, we have a lot of Jews. And we have everything alone. We have school alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were a very special street over there. The street called Jew Street. Why? What every-- over there, almost everybody was living Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was over there. We went to the school. The children went to the [NON-ENGLISH] there. And it was a synagogue over there. And it was a very nice life. But the people don't like us-- the little children-- not the little children where we went to the school. We don't have bus or-- or-- or car. We went in the-- to the school. We take-- we take our little pack, and we went. And the children was-- take stones and throw of us, and tell that we are stink. We are Jewish. And they start to-- to hit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But our parents told us all the time don't-- don't play with them. Don't hit back. But the boys was more fighting. And they sometimes-- they went to fight, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But everybody was afraid from the teachers and from the parents. Because the parents told us all the time don't tend attention. You go in and leave them alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- you understand? When we came to the school, we was only Jew. And the teachers was Jews. So we don't have problem. But we have problem we went to the school, or when we came.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was very funny, you know, what happening when you was-- when you have a neighbor, you know. We was living neighbor-- Jew and-- and the gentiles. And is the gentiles in this time, they have a lot of children, like-- like-- like Jewish. Or they don't have, you know-- they-- they have children-- if they have 10 children-- like-- like us. And if we was neighbors, they was very nice to us, the neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if we went to the other street, they was mean to us. You-- you are? So we was playing with the-- you know-- we was-- everybody has a little house. And everybody has a big-- we had-- but I remember, we have a very, very big yard outside-- a very big yard. And we was playing in the-- in the summertime. We was playing only outside and not in the-- in the room, and not in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was clean and nice. And we was only outside. And we was playing a lot outside in the yard. And we was going to the gentile children, and they coming to us. So we was very friendly-- only the neighbors. But after the neighbors, they was very mean. I remember in-- in-- I remember a little in '43 when-- when my-- my-- my mother-- in '43-- I don't know if this is-- I think in-- in-- in '43-- when my mother get-- when he hurt-- that-- the-- the big brother, in Yugoslavia-- takes him and all the family. One day they take all them outside. And they killed them-- five minute. The mother was very young. He was-- he was pregnant. He was sick. I shows you-- I show you the picture. And it was six children with her. And they take-- I think the German, they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was one of your half-brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is half-brothers, yeah-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he killed as well? Was your half-brother killed with his family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they took-- they came all the time. They took some of the mens away. And they left the children. And the woman arrived with they. The mother heard this. She don't know what to do. She was very afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and she was with little children. And she don't know what to do. And the big children from the half-sisters, they were all almost married. And she was [NON-ENGLISH]. And then my father was disappeared. So we don't know what-- what happened with the father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stand with the mother. And Hannah was the-- the biggest, and David was-- and Yeshi-- no. In-- yeah-- yeah-- I-- I-- jump, because in '43-- I don't remember exactly when. They took my-- my brother, Yeshi. They took away.

speaker: interviewee
text: David stay us-- with us, because he was-- he was look like very young. He was very young. But he was young, and he was little one, short one, and very little one. So he looked like-- like a child. And Hannah was with us. And Hannah-- my mother was spoking only with Hannah, because she was the biggest. And she thought, she don't know what to do with the children. She's afraid that something is a very bad situation is going.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I want to tell you what happens, that we have a very nice family-- an old family. A-- a old woman and man is across on the street, says they love us very much. And they was helping us very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very special man. He was in the-- the area. He was-- he working. And he came to us to tell us that something is going here very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was a gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a gentile. She was helping. He-- she-- the woman-- came in the morning, in 4 o'clock in the morning. She was bringing-- bringing us food, in '43. Because our situation was not good after the-- our-- father-- we don't know what happened. So we left little children with mother. And she was helping us. But there's a man told us that something going-- he-- she want-- she-- she doesn't want to tell us exactly. I don't know. But he want to tell for mother, because he love us very much. They love us very much-- that something is going here very, very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German-- and the-- and the Hungarian people is very bad. They don't want-- they don't want the Jews. They want to take away. And they-- they want to-- to take the-- the-- the houses and everything. Because some people was very rich. And some people was living-- you know? And my mother was very worried. She cannot sleep. She cannot-- she don't know what to do. So they told us that perhaps I can-- they can take me, because they love me very much. They-- they love very much, but they don't have children. And they love me. Sometimes I was going over there and playing, you know? But I was-- I was-- I was free-- whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '43, they give us a sign for my mother that something can happen very, very bad. And it was-- this time was [NON-ENGLISH]. In [NON-ENGLISH]-- and you know the-- the German was moving to Italia and to Czechia, to Yugoslavia without fight-- without fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and Hungaria-- they come too-- later they come. In '42, they was-- they was in-- in Hungarian but without-- without war, you know? But they-- when-- when the people-- like-- like-- you know, if the people going to the synagogue, or they went with the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], with the bears, and you know the people went with [NON-ENGLISH] outside, and with payot. You know what is payot?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people start to make [NON-ENGLISH]-- make problems. And this people, this gentile people-- the man that worked in the-- area, that know everything, that something is very wrong, that they can make for the Jewish people something very wrong. They told for my mother that they can take me to-- to hide me. I don't know nothing about this. My mother was afraid to tell me. I don't want to leave nobody. But he-- he want help for her-- herself to do something. And he-- he-- he felt my mother was felt that if she can take somebody, and to save somebody, perhaps she can do it something. So she take my mother. And she-- they told the plan, what they want to do with me-- to behind me-- to behind. And my mother afraid to tell for the little children too, because the little children can speak-- speak out. And the gentile was near to us-- you know, this side and this side. Like we live here, and the other side is gentile. The other side is gentile. And they start in '43-- right away they-- they start to be mean, even the-- even the-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Neighbors.

speaker: interviewee
text: --neighbors. And she was afraid too. There I was afraid, because everybody know what going on in-- in our little town-- everybody. They know what going on. And when-- when the woman was coming to us in 4 o'clock in the night-- in the morning, he was hiding herself with a big-- like a-- a jacket, and put the food that nobody can see. Or in the night-- very late in the night-- she brought us some food. And she tried to help me-- and not only me, help for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember Pater. Her name was Pater. Not Peter like you-- Pater. Only that this is her family name, Peter-- Pater. They was very old, very nice people. And they-- and they start to tell me that I can go upstairs, and perhaps I stay over there. And they help me. And you know, I was over there every time. Not only I, I went over there, my sister too. But they take care of me, very special. Because sometimes, he went-- he went to Budapest, you know, and to buy some-- all kind of-- no-- present. He bring me only for me present-- a little dress, a little-- and all kind of toys. And you know, over there, the toys was very expensive. Even we have-- we was a little children, we don't get a little-- we don't play with-- with all kind of toys. We don't get. I remember that there is a big sisters have two, three very nice books. And before we went to sleep, they was reading us the book. I remember the little-- red-- the-- the wall with the red girls that go to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Red Riding Hood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember the-- I remember the little-- a few-- I remember a few little nice stories. But you know, they take the-- the books-- the sisters-- and they take care of the-- the books. But they was very nice to us and very gentle. And they was sitting near the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know what? When I get the presents, the present was every time in their room, not in my room. And sometimes I take the sisters. And I thought, I have a lot of toys that I want to share. So I came. And the toys was sitting like this. I was played with this, but the toys and the books was like from the-- from the stores. Because was everything very expensive, and when you have so much children, you have to give them food and everything else, and not only toys.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you can buy for the children a lot of things. And you buy the children the TV-- the children hear radios. The children go-- they have video. They have a lot of toys. They have books, and they do whatever they want-- not in my age, and not in my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now to go back for a minute, what were the circumstances under which your father left the family? You talk now about how he was already gone. Can you tell me how it is that he came to leave the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before he was living?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. When--

speaker: interviewee
text: When we was living-- when he was alive?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. We he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a good situation. And it was a good [NON-ENGLISH]. And he can take care of us. But you know, in this time, the-- the important things was to teach the children. The boys was-- need to go to the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the girls, after we start the-- the first grade, to go to [NON-ENGLISH] and to learn, and to-- only to play a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he taken? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know. You know, all the-- the fathers-- I told you. They take-- take away. They take away. They have a special place they was working. We don't know. So I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so when the Nazis came in, the men of the community were taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was living with us. And then he take of-- care of us, whatever she can do in this time. You know, in this time, usually the-- the woman was at home-- take care of the children. Almost every year they was pregnant-- every time was babies. And it was a lot of work at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when the children grow up? The children was helping to the mother. The-- the boys was in the [NON-ENGLISH], because the boys need to learn the Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls, no. The girls, no-- yes. We know-- we know the little prays, you know, like the berakhot, and the-- when-- where you get up in the morning, you have a special pray, like modeh ani. And we going to-- when you going to sleep, you have a little pray-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And you have all the berakhot before you going to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we need everything. And we know everything. But from the boys, they take more-- they need to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] to learn the Torah. So when this family was helping you, did--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but this is not-- yes, they was helping a little bit. They cannot-- they helping every time a little bit-- not with-- not with money, only with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have a lot of children. What is food? Food you need every time. You are eating three, four times a day. You are eating all the time. And when you are little children, you want to eat all the time. And did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when-- when you are hungry, you want to eat more. When you are-- where-- when you know that you can't take the food, you are not hungry. Because you know that after five minute, you can eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to change tapes now.
